one_line_takeaway: PoC-Adapt pairs a pre/post system-state 'semantic oracle' with an offline-trained DDQN action-selection policy to raise real-world automated CVE PoC reproduction from FaultLine's 12% to 15-17.5% success rate while roughly halving exploration steps, but environment setup (60.76% conditional failure) and subtle vulnerability classes (0% on deserialization) remain the dominant bottlenecks.
key_finding: On FL-Bench-100, PoC-Adapt reaches 15.0% success rate (SR) vs FaultLine's 12.0% (a 25% relative improvement), while halving Time-to-Exploit (16.33 vs 35.92 steps) and more than doubling Exploit Efficiency (0.025 vs 0.011). On the real-world GHSA-Real80 set, PoC-Adapt reproduces 12/80 vulnerabilities (15% SR) at an average $0.42 and 7.14 minutes per successful reproduction (320.2k tokens), vs $0.64/13.87 min/499.2k tokens for failed attempts; the Planner (environment-setup) stage is the dominant bottleneck with a 60.76% conditional failure rate (48/79), and the Validator filters out 55.56% of candidates. SR varies sharply by vulnerability class: 23.1% for Command Injection (CWE-78), 22.2% for XSS (CWE-79), 16.7% for Path Traversal (CWE-22), but 0% for Deserialization (CWE-502); by severity, 20.7% (Critical), 17.9% (High), 4.3% (Medium). The RL ablation shows DDQN vs. a random-action baseline on the held-out FL-Bench-100 test set: 44.83% vs 38.86% SRpolicy, 22.19 vs 40.73 TTEpolicy steps, 0.0427 vs 0.0084 EEpolicy. On GHSA-Real80, adding the RL policy raises SR from 15.0% to 17.5% (+16.7% relative) with a 23% token-cost increase (320.2k to 393.4k). Backend robustness: Gemini-2.5-Pro gives the best SR (15%) at highest cost ($0.61/12.86 min avg on GHSA-Real80); DeepSeek-V3 cuts cost to $0.07 (3.59 min) but SR drops to 6.25%; Qwen-3 gives 11.25% SR at $0.13 but takes 15.65 min.
core_contribution: PoC-Adapt, an end-to-end LLM multi-agent framework for automated proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit generation and verification from vulnerability reports (CVE/GHSA-ID + natural-language description). The paper's own novel contributions (not related work) are: (1) a Semantic Oracle that validates exploits by having a Validator agent capture pre-execution and post-execution system-state snapshots (file hashes, env vars, DB records, etc.) and using an LLM-as-judge to check whether the observed state delta matches an explicit 'impact hypothesis' produced by the Exploiter agent, replacing crash/flag/exit-code based validation; and (2) an Adaptive Policy Learning module that formulates the Exploiter agent's tool-selection process as an MDP (13-feature state space, 9-action space) and trains a Double Deep Q-Network (DDQN) offline on replayed exploitation-log trajectories to recommend top-k next actions, bounding the LLM's tool-calling search space. These two mechanisms are wrapped in a 5-stage, 4-agent pipeline (Root Cause Analyzer, Planner/Environment Setup, Exploiter, Validator) built on LangGraph/LangChain with Gemini-2.5-Pro as the default backend.
threat_model: Defensive/offensive-security tooling threat model, not an attack-on-AI paper: the system acts as an automated red-team/PoC-generation agent for already-disclosed, already-patched CVEs/GHSA advisories, to help security teams and OSS maintainers validate exploitability and patches faster. All experiments run in isolated, least-privilege Docker sandboxes with no production systems or unpatched vulnerabilities touched. The paper explicitly discusses dual-use risk of its own exploit-generation capability being weaponized by malicious actors, and lists mitigations it built in (controlled/sandboxed tool access, bounded refinement iterations B=3, semantic state-matching oracle) rather than treating the LLM itself as an attack surface (no prompt-injection/jailbreak/model-security angle is present).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Planner/environment-setup is the dominant real-world failure mode (60.76% conditional failure on GHSA-Real80) due to heterogeneous build systems, dependency conflicts, and outdated docs, often exceeding the 60-min/$1 budget; (2) the Semantic Oracle degrades sharply on subtle/context-dependent vulnerabilities -- 0% SR on CWE-502 deserialization and 4.3% on Medium-severity cases -- due to the fixed refinement budget (B=3) causing overly conservative false negatives; (3) strong dependence on high-capability (expensive) LLMs -- swapping to DeepSeek-V3 cuts SR to 6.25%; (4) the DDQN policy is trained on only 86 trajectories from FL-Bench-100, limiting generalization to the more diverse GHSA-Real80 distribution; (5) the oracle's state profiling may miss delayed/out-of-band effects (e.g., network exfiltration) not captured by the predefined state snapshot, risking false negatives; (6) baseline comparison limited to FaultLine only, since CVE-Genie/PTFusion have no public reproducible implementations aligned with the benchmark setup; results reflect performance under strict resource constraints ($1/60min/3 loops) rather than an unconstrained upper bound.

datasets_used: FL-Bench-100 (100 vulnerabilities: 70 from CWE-Bench-Java, 30 from PrimeVul; CWE-22/78/79/94)
GHSA-Real80 (80 real-world GitHub Security Advisories, 73 repos, 7 languages, 9 CWE categories)
RL training split of FL-Bench-100: 75 CVEs/86 episodes train+val, 56 CVEs/59 episodes test
quantitative_results: FL-Bench-100: PoC-Adapt SR 15.0% vs FaultLine SR 12.0% (25% relative gain)
GHSA-Real80: 12/80 vulnerabilities reproduced, SR 15.0%, avg cost $0.42 / 7.14 min / 320.2k tokens per success
GHSA-Real80: Planner-stage conditional failure rate 60.76% (48/79); Exploiter failure 12.90%; Validator rejects 55.56% of candidates
GHSA-Real80 SR by CWE: Command Injection (CWE-78) 23.1%, XSS (CWE-79) 22.2%, Path Traversal (CWE-22) 16.7%, Deserialization (CWE-502) 0%
GHSA-Real80 SR by severity: Critical 20.7%, High 17.9%, Medium 4.3%
RL held-out test (FL-Bench-100): DDQN SRpolicy 44.83% vs random-agent 38.86%±4.18; TTEpolicy 22.19 vs 40.73±0.99 steps; EEpolicy 0.0427 vs 0.0084±0.0010
GHSA-Real80 ablation: SR 17.5% with RL vs 15.0% without RL (+16.7% relative); TTE 19.43 vs 20.67 steps; tokens 393.4k vs 320.2k; EE 0.052 vs 0.048
Backend comparison on GHSA-Real80: Gemini-2.5-Pro SR 15.0% ($0.61, 12.86 min avg); DeepSeek-V3 SR 6.25% ($0.07, 3.59 min); Qwen-3 SR 11.25% ($0.13, 15.65 min)
baselines_compared: FaultLine (LLM-based hierarchical/data-flow reasoning system; primary end-to-end SOTA baseline on FL-Bench-100)
Random agent averaged over 100 seeds (baseline for the DDQN policy-learning ablation)
Alternative LLM backends as robustness comparison, not independent systems: Gemini-2.5-Pro (default), DeepSeek-V3, Qwen-3
